introduction.title: Vietnamese Passive Voice
content: Vietnamese passive voice is unique: it splits passives into POSITIVE and NEGATIVE based on whether the action is good or bad for the subject. There's no neutral 'be + verb' form like English.

'Được' is used when the subject benefits or experiences something good: 'Tôi được khen' (I was praised). 'Bị' is used when the subject suffers or experiences something bad: 'Tôi bị mắng' (I was scolded).
Choosing the wrong marker sounds odd or even rude — the speaker's attitude is built into the grammar.

sections.title: Positive Passive (được)
sections.description: Used when something good happens to the subject

notes: Implies a benefit, gift, or positive experience.
Also means 'can/may' as a modal — context decides.
Agent goes between được and verb, or use 'bởi' (by) after.
Combines with tense: 'đã được' (was), 'sẽ được' (will be).
I was punished: Tôi ~~được~~ __bị__ phạt (punishment is negative — use bị).
As a modal verb, 'được' also means 'can/may' (see Modal Verbs module).

examples.vietnamese: Tôi đã được chọn làm trưởng nhóm
examples.english: I was chosen as team leader
examples.literalEnglish: I already got choose as team-leader

examples.notes: 'được phép' — state of having permission, no specific agent

sections.title: Negative Passive (bị)
sections.description: Used when something bad happens to the subject

notes: Implies suffering, harm, or negative experience.
Also used for undesirable states: 'bị bệnh' (be sick), 'bị hỏng' (be broken).
I was praised: Tôi ~~bị~~ __được__ khen (praise is positive — use được; bị here sounds sarcastic).

examples.notes: 'bị' + state (no verb action, just undesirable condition)

examples.vietnamese: Tôi bị bố mẹ bắt ở nhà
examples.english: I was forced by my parents to stay home
examples.literalEnglish: I suffered parents force stay home
examples.notes: Compare: 'được bố mẹ cho đi' (allowed to go — positive) vs 'bị bố mẹ bắt ở nhà' (forced to stay — negative)
